original_hypothesis.title: Fee-on-Transfer Tokens Cause Accounting Discrepancy in Outbound Transfers
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: When transferOutV5 is called for a fee-on-transfer token, the router transfers 'amount' to the recipient, who receives 'amount - fee'. However, the TransferOut event emits the full 'amount'. Bifrost observes this event and records that the full amount was delivered, creating an accounting discrepancy.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: THORChain explicitly aims to support 'weird' ERC20s including fee-on-transfer tokens. If the outbound accounting is flawed, users will be shortchanged and the protocol's internal state will diverge from actual EVM state.
deployment_assumptions: Protocol lists fee-on-transfer tokens (e.g., USDT with fees enabled)

proof_of_signal.pattern: Emits TransferOut with transferOutPayload.amount instead of actual transferred amount

preconditions: User requests an outbound swap for this token

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: Event emission does not use balance-before/balance-after check
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Normal protocol operation results in loss of funds for the user relative to THORChain's accounting
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Protocol retains the fee difference internally, user loses out

economic_questions: Does THORChain currently support any active fee-on-transfer tokens?
How does Bifrost reconcile fee-on-transfer discrepancies?
validation_requirements: Deploy a mock fee-on-transfer token and test transferOutV5
Verify Bifrost parsing logic for TransferOut events

potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Loss equal to the token's transfer fee percentage
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Proportional to outbound volume of the affected token

validation_result.impact_summary: For fee-on-transfer tokens, outbound transfer events emit the requested amount rather than the actual amount received by the recipient, causing Bifrost to record incorrect outbound amounts and creating a divergence between THORChain's internal state and on-chain reality.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The hypothesis correctly identifies that `_transferOutV5` and `transferOut` do not account for fee-on-transfer token mechanics on outbound transfers. During deposits, the router correctly uses a balance-before/balance-after pattern via `safeTransferFrom` to measure the actual tokens received. However, during outbound transfers, the code simply passes the requested `amount` to `transfer()` and emits the same `amount` in the `TransferOut` event without checking how much the recipient actually received. For tokens like USDT (which has a configurable `basisPointsRate` fee), the recipient receives `amount - fee` while the event reports the full `amount`. This is an asymmetric handling of fee-on-transfer tokens: correct on inbound, incorrect on outbound.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The defect is clearly visible by comparing the inbound deposit path (which uses balance-diff accounting) with the outbound transfer paths (which do not). In `_deposit`, `safeTransferFrom` measures actual received tokens. In `_transferOutV5` and `transferOut`, the raw requested amount is used for event emission and allowance deduction without any balance-diff check.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: The event emits `transferOutPayload.amount` (the requested amount), not the actual amount received by the recipient after any fee-on-transfer deductions. No balance-before/balance-after check is performed on outbound transfers.
safeAmount is set to the raw `amount` parameter without checking the actual received amount at the destination, causing the same accounting discrepancy.

The inbound path correctly uses balance-before/balance-after to determine actual received amount. This asymmetry with the outbound path confirms the defect is an oversight rather than intentional design.

calling_sequence: 1. Vault (msg.sender) calls transferOutV5(transferOutPayload) with a fee-on-transfer token
4. For ERC20 path: _vaultAllowance[msg.sender][asset] -= amount (full amount deducted)
5. asset.call(transfer(to, amount)) - token transfer initiated for full amount
6. Fee-on-transfer token deducts fee internally; recipient receives (amount - fee)
7. emit TransferOut(..., transferOutPayload.amount, ...) - event emits full amount
8. Bifrost observes TransferOut event and records full amount as delivered
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The defect occurs at step 7: the event uses `transferOutPayload.amount` which is the pre-fee amount. The actual tokens received by the recipient are `amount - fee`. There is no balance-diff check on the outbound path. The same pattern affects `transferOut` (V4), `_transferOutAndCallV5` (ERC20 path), and the equivalent functions in `AvaxRouter.sol`.

deployment_assumptions_checked: Protocol lists fee-on-transfer tokens (e.g., USDT with fees enabled)
config_validation.deployment_evidence: The deployment scripts (deploy.ts) only deploy AvaxRouter and AvaxAggregator. They do not specify which tokens are whitelisted or listed by the protocol. The USDT.sol contract in scope has fee-on-transfer capability (basisPointsRate, maximumFee set via setParams). Currently USDT fees are 0 on mainnet, but the mechanism exists and could be enabled by USDT's owner at any time.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: This is not an active attack but rather a systemic accounting error. When THORChain lists a fee-on-transfer token and processes outbound swaps, every outbound transfer will report more tokens delivered than actually received. Over time, this creates a growing discrepancy between THORChain's internal accounting and on-chain reality. Users are shortchanged by the fee amount on every outbound transaction, and THORChain's solvency tracking becomes inaccurate.
validation_result.impact_assessment: MEDIUM severity. The code contains a real defect in asymmetric fee-on-transfer handling between inbound (correct) and outbound (incorrect) paths. Impact materializes when fee-on-transfer tokens are used. The vault's allowance is decremented by the full amount, meaning the router's actual token balance decreases by the full amount (sent) minus only what the fee-on-transfer mechanism retains in the router (which is 0 - the fee goes to the token owner). This means allowance and actual balance stay in sync at the router level, but the event emitted misrepresents what the user received, causing Bifrost to record incorrect delivery amounts.
recommended_mitigations: Add balance-before/balance-after check on outbound ERC20 transfers in _transferOutV5 and transferOut, similar to safeTransferFrom used on inbound
Emit the actual transferred amount (balance delta at recipient or balance delta at router) in TransferOut events instead of the requested amount
Consider adding a safeTransferTo helper function that mirrors safeTransferFrom for outbound transfers
Document explicitly which token types are supported and add integration tests for fee-on-transfer token outbound flows
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy a mock fee-on-transfer ERC20 token with a 1% transfer fee. 2. Deploy THORChain_Router. 3. Deposit tokens to the router via depositWithExpiry (observe correct balance-diff accounting). 4. Call transferOutV5 for 1000 tokens. 5. Verify recipient received ~990 tokens. 6. Check TransferOut event emits 1000 (not 990). 7. Confirm the accounting discrepancy.
uncertainties: Whether THORChain currently lists any fee-on-transfer tokens in production
How Bifrost handles the discrepancy in practice - it may have off-chain compensating logic
USDT fees are currently 0 on mainnet; the defect only manifests if fees are enabled or another fee-on-transfer token is listed
additional_testing_needed: End-to-end test with a fee-on-transfer token through the full deposit -> transferOut cycle
Verify Bifrost event parsing logic to confirm whether it uses emitted amount directly or has compensating checks
Test the same scenario in AvaxRouter.sol transferOut which has the identical pattern
